---
title: "Why Facility Decision Space Matters for Chronic Care in Rural Kenya"
subtitle: "What my accepted BMC Public Health study found about medicine stockouts, budgets, and the politics of primary care"
author: "Nichodemus Amollo"
date: "2026-03-13"
categories: [Public Health, Health Financing, Health Systems, NCDs, Kenya]
image: "/img/blog-health.svg"
---

BMC Public Health has accepted my first-author manuscript on the financial determinants of hypertension and diabetes care in rural primary health facilities in Kisumu County. That acceptance is a major academic milestone for me, but it also matters for a larger reason: the study captures a policy contradiction that health workers in rural Kenya deal with every day.

Clinicians can diagnose hypertension. They can write the prescription. They can explain follow-up. But if the facility cannot spend quickly when medicines run out, the treatment pathway breaks down long before the science does.

::: {.research-abstract}
**Share-ready abstract:** This study examined how financing arrangements shape hypertension and diabetes care in seven public primary health facilities in Seme Sub-County, Kisumu. Using a convergent parallel mixed-methods design, I combined facility questionnaires, retrospective financial record review, and interviews with facility in-charges. The study found that all facilities prepared plans and budgets, but none had a dedicated NCD budget line, none had full direct spending autonomy, and 85.7 percent reported frequent medicine stockouts. The main implication is that chronic-care continuity depends not only on funding volume, but on facility decision space: the ability to use available funds quickly, locally, and accountably when patient care is at risk.
:::

## The paradox at the center of the study

At first glance, the problem can look like ordinary underfunding. Kenya's NCD burden is growing, and primary care facilities are already stretched. But the field evidence pointed to something more specific.

All seven facilities in this study had annual workplans and budgets. All had bank accounts. Yet none had full formal autonomy to spend funds directly when urgent needs emerged. That means a facility could identify an NCD stockout, know where to buy the required medicine locally, and still be forced to wait for approval from a higher administrative level.

That is the paradox: a system can appear financially organized on paper while remaining operationally paralyzed in practice.

## What the data showed

Several findings kept repeating across the quantitative records and the interviews:

- 71.4 percent of facilities depended mainly on NHIF reimbursements and donor support.
- Only 28.6 percent received direct county funding.
- 57.1 percent relied on just two active funding streams.
- None had a dedicated NCD budget line.
- None had full direct spending autonomy.
- 85.7 percent reported frequent medication stockouts.

Taken together, those figures describe a system with narrow revenue options, weak protection for chronic-care budgets, and very limited local ability to respond when routine supply channels fail.

## Why this is a governance problem, not only a supply problem

It is tempting to describe medicine stockouts as a logistics issue. Sometimes they are. But in this study, stockouts were also a financing-governance issue.

When approval for spending takes weeks, a facility cannot bridge a gap in central supply. When NCD care has no protected budget line, it competes with every other immediate operational pressure. When reimbursements are delayed, planning becomes performative rather than actionable.

This is why I argue that the sustainability of chronic care depends on **facility decision space**, not only on the total amount of money in the system. If the purse strings remain too far from the point of care, patients experience that distance as missed refills, interrupted treatment, and preventable complications.

## Why the finding matters beyond Seme

The study was conducted in seven facilities in Seme Sub-County, so it should not be read as a statistical description of every county in Kenya. But the policy mechanisms it identifies are transferable.

Many decentralized health systems face the same tension:

- central control is used to protect accountability
- frontline facilities are responsible for service delivery
- chronic disease care requires continuity rather than one-off response

That combination creates friction. The deeper lesson from this work is that accountability systems must be designed so that they do not disable the very care they are supposed to safeguard.

## Why this matters now

Kenya's transition from NHIF to the Social Health Authority raises an obvious question: will new financing reforms improve the timeliness, predictability, and local usability of funds for chronic care?

My accepted paper provides a baseline for answering that question. It documents what the system looked like before the new financing environment was fully operational. That matters because reforms are too often judged by legal intent or national announcements rather than by what changes at the facility counter.

The real test is practical:

1. Can facilities access money faster when essential NCD medicines run out?
2. Are NCD budgets becoming more visible and more protected?
3. Are county approvals becoming quicker and more predictable?
4. Do patients experience fewer interruptions in treatment?

Those are the questions I want the next phase of my thesis and future doctoral research to keep following.

## Where this research is taking me

This manuscript is not the end of the story. It has sharpened my research interests around four connected themes:

- health financing and facility-level decision space
- hypertension and diabetes care in rural primary health systems
- mixed-methods health systems research
- community-grounded pathways for better chronic disease management

The thesis I am finalizing now is part of a broader research direction I want to keep building: evidence that is academically rigorous, operationally useful, and close enough to frontline reality to change actual decisions.

## Short abstract for applications or social sharing

**Abstract:** This study examined financial determinants of hypertension and diabetes care in seven rural public primary health facilities in Seme Sub-County, Kisumu County, Kenya. Using a convergent parallel mixed-methods design, it combined structured facility questionnaires, retrospective financial record review, and interviews with facility in-charges. All facilities prepared plans and budgets, but none had a dedicated NCD budget line and none had full direct spending autonomy despite holding bank accounts. Most relied on narrow and unstable funding streams, and 85.7 percent reported frequent medicine stockouts. The findings suggest that chronic-care continuity depends not only on the volume of financing but on whether frontline facilities have sufficient decision space to use funds quickly and accountably when patient care is at risk.

## Social media caption

My first-author manuscript on financing constraints and chronic NCD care in rural Kenya has been accepted in *BMC Public Health*. The core finding is simple: facilities can have plans, bank accounts, and trained staff, but if they cannot spend quickly when medicines run out, continuity of care still collapses. I wrote a plain-language essay on why facility decision space matters for hypertension and diabetes care, and why this should shape the next phase of health-financing reform.
